description.s: A Hob view pattern for building interactive UIs with local state that update efficiently without triggering kernel re-renders.

Hobson's rendering system re-evaluates a view's entire Hob code to produce a new DOM tree, then uses morphdom to diff it against the existing DOM. This works well for data-driven views, but is expensive and lossy for views with rich local state:
: Re-evaluation means re-querying all items from the database, rebuilding all data structures, and re-running all logic — even if only one tab changed.
: Even with morphdom diffing, a full re-render resets local state that lives outside the DOM: atom values, scroll positions in sibling panels, text in filter inputs, focus state.
: If the view uses atoms for local state (e.g. which tab is selected), and atom mutations trigger re-renders, the view re-renders itself infinitely.
The Solution: Build Once, Update Surgically
Split the view into two phases:
to create a stable outer DOM structure with named refs. This runs once, at initial evaluation.
to replace the children of specific ref'd containers. Call these imperatively when local state changes.

;; Phase 1: Stable skeleton (runs once)
(let [state (atom "tab-a")
      [root refs] (hiccup->dom+
        [:div {}
          [:div {:ref :tabs}]
          [:div {:ref :content}]])
      tabs-el (:tabs refs)
      content-el (:content refs)

      ;; Phase 2: Builder functions
      build-tabs! (fn []
        (dom-set-content! tabs-el
          (for [t ["tab-a" "tab-b"]]
            [:div {:style (if (= t (deref state)) "font-weight:bold" "")
                   :on-click (fn [e]
                               (reset! state t)
                               (build-tabs!)
                               (build-content!))}
              t])))
      build-content! (fn []
        (dom-set-content! content-el
          [:div {} (str "Showing: " (deref state))]))]

  ;; Initial render
  (build-tabs!)
  (build-content!)
  root)
Morphdom is the kernel's tool for diffing between full re-renders. It operates at the wrong level for intra-view updates:
Re-evaluate all Hob code, re-query DB

doesn't diff — it clears and replaces children of a specific container. This is efficient because each builder only touches a small subtree, and sibling containers (with their scroll positions, input values, etc.) are untouched.
Because builder functions often call each other (clicking a tab rebuilds both the tab bar and the content), and Hob's
bindings are sequential, use atoms for forward declaration:

(let [build-tabs!    (atom nil)
      build-content! (atom nil)

      _ (reset! build-tabs!
          (fn [] ... ((deref build-content!)) ...))

      _ (reset! build-content!
          (fn [] ...))
  ...)
, which isn't defined yet at the point
For this pattern to work, local atom mutations must NOT trigger a kernel re-render. The evaluator tags atoms with their creation context (
), and the rendering system skips dependency tracking for self-owned atoms. This means
inside an event handler updates the atom's value without the kernel knowing — exactly what we want.
If this mechanism breaks, the symptom is an infinite re-render loop: atom reset triggers re-render, re-render re-creates atoms, which triggers another re-render.
where switching tabs shouldn't re-query everything
where typing in a filter should only update the list, not the whole view
Any view with local interactive state
For simple views that just display data and have no local state, a plain hiccup return (letting the kernel handle re-renders via morphdom) is simpler and sufficient.
is the canonical example. It has:
A stable skeleton with refs for each panel
— The kernel-level counterpart: saving data without re-rendering
